request.question: Who did A.L. "Doodle" Owens work with to write many songs for the singer of "He Stopped Loving Her Today"?

title: A.L. "Doodle" Owens
I Can't Believe That You've Stopped Loving Me
(I'm So) Afraid of Losing You Again
I Never Once Stopped Loving You (song)
Wine Colored Roses (song)
All I Have to Offer You (Is Me)
The Right Left Hand
Somebody Wants Me Out of the Way
George Jones
Fourteen Minutes Old
sentences: Arthur Leo "Doodle" Owens (November 28, 1930 – October 4, 1999) was an American country music songwriter and singer.

He had a long songwriting partnership with Dallas Frazier, with whom he wrote "All I Have to Offer You (Is Me)" (1969), "(I'm So) Afraid of Losing You Again" (1969), "I Can't Believe That You've Stopped Loving Me" (1970) and "Then Who Am I" (1974), all number-one country hits for Charlie Pride.
In the 1980s, Owens wrote many songs with fellow songwriter Dennis Knutson for George Jones and other artists.
"I Can't Believe That You've Stopped Loving Me" is a song written by Dallas Frazier and A.L. "Doodle" Owens, and recorded by American country music artist Charley Pride.
It was released in September 1970 as the second single from the album "From Me to You".
"I Can't Believe That You've Stopped Loving Me" was Charley Pride's fifth number one on the country charts.
The single stayed at number one for two weeks and spent a total of fifteen weeks on the country charts.
"(I'm So) Afraid of Losing You Again" is a song written by Dallas Frazier and A.L. "Doodle" Owens, and recorded by American country music artist Charley Pride.
It was released in October 1969 as the first single from the album "Just Plain Charley". "
(I'm So) Afraid of Losing You Again" was Charley Pride's second number one on the country charts.
The single stayed at number one for three weeks and spent a total of 15 weeks on the country charts.
"I Never Once Stopped Loving You" is a single by American country music artist Connie Smith.
Released in March 1970, the song reached #5 on the "Billboard" Hot Country Singles chart.
An album of the same name was released in September 1970 that included the song.
In addition, "I Never Once Stopped Loving You" also peaked at #17 on the Canadian "RPM" Country Tracks chart.
"Wine Colored Roses" is a song written by Dennis Knutson and A.L. "Doodle" Owens, recorded by American country music artist George Jones.
It was released in September 1986 as the first single and title track from Jones' album "Wine Colored Roses".
The song peaked at number 10 on the "Billboard" Hot Country Singles chart.
The song tells the story of a man who receives a letter from a caring ex-lover who asks him if he still drinks.
Unable to overcome his own shame to speak with her on the telephone, he sends her a dozen wine colored roses as a symbol that he still drinks.

The song became part of Jones' live show at the time, and when he would sing the line, "She asked if I had quit drinkin'," audiences would often cheer, mindful of how George had gotten sober himself in 1984.

In his essay for the liner notes to the 1994 Sony compilation "The Essential George Jones: The Spirit of Country", Rich Kienzle states, "If there were any doubters, 'Wine Colored Roses' proved Jones was a timeless superstar, even without stimulants."
"All I Have to Offer You (Is Me)" is a song written by Dallas Frazier and A.L. "Doodle" Owens, and recorded by American country music artist Charley Pride.
It was released in June 1969 as the first single from the album "The Best of Charley Pride".
"The Right Left Hand" is a song written by Dennis Knutson and A.L. "Doodle" Owens, and recorded by American country music artist George Jones.
It was released in January 1987 as the second single from his album "Wine Colored Roses".
The song peaked at number 8 on the "Billboard" Hot Country Singles chart.
The song is about marriage; the narrator rejoices at putting "a golden band on the right left hand" after several failed marriages.
Many interpreted the song as George's tribute to his fourth wife Nancy, whom he credited with saving his life and career.
Jones would not see the Top 10 again until late 1988.
"Somebody Wants Me Out of the Way" is a song written by Dennis Knutson and A.L. "Doodle" Owens and recorded by American country music artist George Jones.
It was released in April 1986 as the third single from his album "Who's Gonna Fill Their Shoes".
The song peaked at number 9 on the "Billboard" Hot Country Singles chart.

The song is very much in the tradition of George's previous hits "Still Doin' Time" and "If Drinkin' Don't Kill Me (Her Memory Will)" in that it is a hard "drinking song," but it's also a "cheatin'" song, with the narrator suspicious that "Somebody keeps paying my bar tab but the bartender won't tell me who."
Jones delivers a soulful, bluesy vocal over a stone country arrangement with a prominent, shimmering acoustic guitar.
The singer, who had been a notorious drinker, was sober by this time thanks to his wife Nancy, who had gotten his career back on track after years of mismanagement and missed concert dates.
George Glenn Jones (September 12, 1931 – April 26, 2013) was an American musician, singer and songwriter.
He achieved international fame for his long list of hit records, including his best known song "He Stopped Loving Her Today", as well as his distinctive voice and phrasing.
For the last twenty years of his life, Jones was frequently referred to as the greatest living country singer.

Country music scholar Bill C. Malone writes, "For the two or three minutes consumed by a song, Jones immerses himself so completely in its lyrics, and in the mood it conveys, that the listener can scarcely avoid becoming similarly involved."
Waylon Jennings expressed a similar opinion in his song "It's Alright": "If we all could sound like we wanted to, we'd all sound like George Jones."
The shape of his nose and facial features earned Jones the nickname "The Possum."
"Fourteen Minutes Old" is a song written by A.L. "Doodle" Owens and Dennis Knutson, and recorded by American country music artist Doug Stone.
It was released in June 1990 as the second single from his self titled debut album.
It peaked at number 6 on the "Billboard" Hot Country Singles & Tracks chart and number 5 on The Canadian RPM Tracks chart.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- If summary_1 already includes explicit profession/role/descriptor tokens for an anchor (for example, 'singer', 'director', 'country music artist'), omit those redundant descriptor tokens and emit a compact anchor-only query consisting of the core visible anchors (names, titles, groups). Retain relation tokens only when the question explicitly requests them or when disambiguation rules demand them. Do not remove descriptors when the question explicitly asks for role/occupation evidence or when a short-title disambiguation rule requires adding an entertainment disambiguator.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Disambiguation for short/ambiguous title tokens:
- If an anchor in summary_1 is a short or potentially ambiguous title token, do not automatically append an entertainment-domain keyword. Only append a plain entertainment disambiguator keyword (for example: film movie album song novel author director) when the original question itself contains explicit entertainment-role words (e.g., film, movie, album, song, directed, author, novella) or when the question explicitly lists multiple title-like tokens. In those cases, include the visible anchor(s) from summary_1 plus the appropriate entertainment keyword to bias retrieval. If the question lacks entertainment cues and does not list multiple titles, do not guess or add an entertainment disambiguator—keep the query anchored to the visible tokens only.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- When the context contains a concise bridge claim about a named entity (or otherwise names candidate/bridge entities), treat the context claim as the summary anchor and perform explicit identity-alignment checks before using passage facts. For each entity explicitly named in `context` as a candidate or bridge, do the following: if at least one passage provides an explicit, identity-aligned statement linking that named entity (or a clearly indicated alias) to the asserted relation, append a single brief corroborating sentence that paraphrases that linking fact and references the corroborating passage and also include one short clause for that context-named entity giving the passage-supported relation value (e.g., "Name: [relation value]."); if no passage provides the relation value for that context-named entity, append a short clause stating that no relation value is provided in the evidence (e.g., "Name: no [relation] provided in evidence."). Do not create such clauses for entities that are only mentioned in `passages` and are not named in `context`. Do not append corroboration if the `context` already contains the identical corroborating fact verbatim. Keep these additions short and keep the overall summary compact.
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.
- When the summaries contain both a short single-token alias and, in the later integrated summary (summary_2), a contiguous multi-token full-name literal that explicitly identifies or is explicitly linked to the same person (for example via an identity link or biographical framing in summary_2), prefer and emit that multi-token full-name literal exactly as it appears in summary_2. Do not prefer the longer form unless summary_2 provides an explicit identity link tying the alias to the full-name literal; if no such explicit link is present, follow the base identity-link rules and do not force selection of the longer form.
- Vocal-role single-token normalization (narrow scope): If summary_2 (alone or composed with summary_1 where identity links are explicit) explicitly contains vocal-role evidence — i.e., one or more verbalized vocal-role phrases that clearly denote a singing performer (examples: "lead singer", "lead vocals", "vocalist" as presented in the summaries) — then you may prefer to emit the concise single-token common noun for a person who sings (e.g., "singer") only when that single-token common noun itself appears verbatim in the summaries or when the vocal-role phrases in the summaries unambiguously denote the same person-role. If those explicit lexical licenses are not present, retain the literal role phrasing as given in summary_2 and do not normalize modifiers or drop qualifiers.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

response.hop1_query: Who did A.L. "Doodle" Owens work with to write many songs for the singer of "He Stopped Loving Her Today"?
hop1_docs: A.L. "Doodle" Owens | Arthur Leo "Doodle" Owens (November 28, 1930 – October 4, 1999) was an American country music songwriter and singer.  He had a long songwriting partnership with Dallas Frazier, with whom he wrote "All I Have to Offer You (Is Me)" (1969), "(I'm So) Afraid of Losing You Again" (1969), "I Can't Believe That You've Stopped Loving Me" (1970) and "Then Who Am I" (1974), all number-one country hits for Charlie Pride.  In the 1980s, Owens wrote many songs with fellow songwriter Dennis Knutson for George Jones and other artists.

I Can't Believe That You've Stopped Loving Me | "I Can't Believe That You've Stopped Loving Me" is a song written by Dallas Frazier and A.L. "Doodle" Owens, and recorded by American country music artist Charley Pride.  It was released in September 1970 as the second single from the album "From Me to You".  "I Can't Believe That You've Stopped Loving Me" was Charley Pride's fifth number one on the country charts.  The single stayed at number one for two weeks and spent a total of fifteen weeks on the country charts.

Falling in Love Again (Marvin Gaye song) | "Falling in Love Again" is a 1977 song recorded by singer Marvin Gaye and issued on his 1978 album, "Here, My Dear" album.  The song was another track on the personal album that did not discuss the demise of his first marriage.  Instead of Anna Gaye, the song talked of the other woman in Marvin's life.  Described in "You Can Leave, but It's Going to Cost You" as "that young girl", she was Janis Hunter, whom Gaye had married.  In a solemn but still certain tone, he wanted to be sure that this time his love for Janis will be what he had always wanted.  But as irony would have it, by the time of the album's release, Marvin and Janis' relationship was failing.  By the end of the album's promotion, Janis had split from the singer after nearly two years as a married couple.  They eventually divorced in February 1981.  This song was the last song on "Here, My Dear" with a reprise from the album's "theme song", "When Did You Stop Loving Me, When Did I Stop Loving You", playing soon afterwards putting the album to a close for good.

He Stopped Loving Her Today | "He Stopped Loving Her Today" is a song recorded by American country music artist George Jones.  It has been named in several surveys as the greatest country song of all time.  It was released in April 1980 as the lead single from the album "I Am What I Am".  The song was Jones's first solo No. 1 single in six years.  The melancholy song was written by Bobby Braddock and Curly Putman.  The week after Jones' death the song re-entered the Hot Country Songs chart at No. 21.  As of November 13, 2013, the single has sold 521,000 copies in the United States.  Since 2008 it has been preserved by the Library of Congress into the National Recording Registry.

Put It in a Love Song | "Put It in a Love Song" is a song by American singer Alicia Keys.  The song, written and produced by Keys and Swizz Beatz, features guest vocals by American R&B singer Beyoncé Knowles.  It was originally sent to radio on January 19, 2010 by J Records, as the fourth single from Keys' fourth studio album, "The Element of Freedom" (2009).  However, the full single release was postponed, so that "Un-Thinkable (I'm Ready)" could be pushed forward as the fourth single.  The R&B and soul song, talks about a girl who just wants her lover to write her a song and prove to her he's worth her time.

The Yankee Doodle Boy | "The Yankee Doodle Boy", also well known as "(I'm a) Yankee Doodle Dandy" is a patriotic song from the Broadway musical "Little Johnny Jones" written by George M. Cohan.  The play opened at the Liberty Theater on November 7, 1904.  The play concerns the trials and tribulations of a fictional American jockey, Johnny Jones (based on the real life jockey Tod Sloan), who rides a horse named "Yankee Doodle" in the English Derby.  Cohan incorporates snippets of several popular traditional American songs into his lyrics of this song, as he often did with his songs.  The song was performed by James Cagney in the 1942 film "Yankee Doodle Dandy", in which he played Cohan.

The Bird (Jerry Reed song) | "The Bird" is a song recorded by American country music singer Jerry Reed.  Written by Hal Coleman and Barry Etris, this novelty song contains impressions of Willie Nelson's "Whiskey River" and "On the Road Again;" and George Jones' "He Stopped Loving Her Today."  It was released in October 1982 as the lead single from the album, "The Bird".  The song peaked at No. 2 on the "Billboard magazine" Hot Country Singles chart just before Christmas.
hop1_titles: I Can't Believe That You've Stopped Loving Me
Falling in Love Again (Marvin Gaye song)
Put It in a Love Song

It was released in September 1986 as the first single and title track from Jones' album "Wine Colored Roses".  The song peaked at number 10 on the "Billboard" Hot Country Singles chart.  The song tells the story of a man who receives a letter from a caring ex-lover who asks him if he still drinks.  Unable to overcome his own shame to speak with her on the telephone, he sends her a dozen wine colored roses as a symbol that he still drinks.  The song became part of Jones' live show at the time, and when he would sing the line, "She asked if I had quit drinkin'," audiences would often cheer, mindful of how George had gotten sober himself in 1984.  In his essay for the liner notes to the 1994 Sony compilation "The Essential George Jones: The Spirit of Country", Rich Kienzle states, "If there were any doubters, 'Wine Colored Roses' proved Jones was a timeless superstar, even without stimulants."

Google Doodle | A Google Doodle is a special, temporary alteration of the logo on Google's homepage that is intended to celebrate holidays, events, achievements and people.  The first Google Doodle was in honor of the Burning Man Festival of 1998, and was designed by Larry Page and Sergey Brin to notify users of their absence in case the servers crashed.  Subsequent Google Doodles were designed by an outside contractor, until Page and Brin asked public relations officer Dennis Hwang to design a logo for Bastille Day in 2000.  From that point onward, Doodles have been organized and published by a team of employees termed "Doodlers".
Dennis Owens | Dennis Ray Owens (born February 24, 1960) is a former professional American football player who played nose tackle for five seasons for the New England Patriots.
hop2_titles: Somebody Wants Me Out of the Way
response.summary_2: A.L. "Doodle" Owens worked with songwriter Dennis Knutson. Passages 1 and 3–5 confirm Owens and Dennis Knutson wrote many songs for George Jones; George Jones is the singer who recorded "He Stopped Loving Her Today" (passage 4).
